text.title: Topical Authority Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Topical Authority application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Structure content as topic clusters: one pillar page (broad overview of the topic) supported by 8-30 cluster articles (specific subtopics). The pillar MUST link to every cluster; every cluster MUST link back to the pillar. Missing bidirectional wiring is rejected.
rule.rationale: Google's helpful-content and semantic-relevance systems assess topical coverage; a cluster of 10-30 interlinking articles signals deeper expertise than one highly-optimised page. AI Overviews preferentially cite recognised topical authorities.

rule.statement: Each cluster article MUST answer all PAA questions for its subtopic, cover ≥1 edge case competitors miss, and be refreshed every ≤6 months. Articles that repeat what the pillar already covers without adding depth are rejected as cluster-dilution.
rule.rationale: Google's helpful-content evaluation rewards content that fully satisfies user intent; thin clusters dilute rather than amplify authority.

rule.statement: Link from each cluster article back to the pillar AND to 2-4 related clusters using keyword-rich, descriptive anchor text. Bare "click here" / "this article" anchors are rejected; over-optimised exact-match anchors on external links are also rejected.
rule.rationale: Anchor text reinforces keyword relevance internally without the manipulation risk that triggers external-link penalties; lazy anchors waste a free signal.

rule.statement: The topic-map MUST list the entities (people, products, concepts, places) extracted from the top-10 SERP titles + H2s for the head term, tagged with `mention_count`. Cluster articles collectively MUST cover every entity with `mention_count ≥ 4`. Entity gaps are tracked as a deliberate backlog, not silently skipped.
rule.rationale: Entity coverage is the strongest correlate of topical relevance scoring; missing high-frequency entities is the single biggest reason a cluster ranks below the top-10.

rule.statement: The topic-map artefact MUST name a single owner who publishes on a stated cadence (e.g. 2 articles / week for 12 weeks). Cadence + owner are part of the artefact; "we'll see how it goes" cadences are rejected.
rule.rationale: Topical authority compounds slowly; without a committed cadence the cluster ships incomplete and the authority signal never accumulates.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
